ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for crispy prawn beancurd skin pancake; mapped to nearest prawn food product class. Exact dish is a Singapore R&D original not represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — shrimp and prawn products (deep-fried preparation)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers crustacean-based products; no specific code for prawn-beancurd-skin pancake exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish preparation. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dishes at this granularity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one piece of approximately 50 g, providing around 104 kcal, suitable as a side dish or appetiser alongside a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Crispy Golden Prawn Pancake — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 酥脆腐皮虾饼 Crispy Golden Prawn Pancake (cr_id G00010053). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on wheat starch-based deep-fried snack (spring roll / fried wonton wrapper category, GI ~67)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Crispy Golden Prawn Pancake. The dominant carbohydrate source is wheat starch-based beancurd skin wrapper, deep-fried. The closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database is a wheat starch-based deep-fried snack (spring roll / fried wonton wrapper), with a published GI of approximately 67 (medium). Glycemic load per 50 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (67 × 22.69) / 100 ≈ 15.2, rounded to 15.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content is extremely low at 5 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: With only 5 mg sodium per 100 g, this dish poses minimal hypertensive risk from sodium; however, patients should avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or sweet chilli sauce, which can substantially increase total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (45.4 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g) and medium GI (~67) produces a significant glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one small piece and avoid sweet dipping sauces, as the high refined carbohydrate content from wheat starch with negligible fibre (0.7 g/100 g) and a medium GI of approximately 67 results in a glycaemic load of ~15 per 50 g serving, which can cause meaningful post-prandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and increases oxidative stress; total fat is low (0.38 g/100 g) but fatty acid profile is not fully characterised
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Although total fat is very low at 0.38 g per 100 g, the deep-frying preparation method generates AGEs that promote vascular inflammation and oxidative stress; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and prefer steamed or baked alternatives where available.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; high refined carbohydrate load promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish because deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, and the high refined carbohydrate content (45.4 g/100 g) with negligible fibre drives a glycaemic load that promotes systemic inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Golden Prawn Pancake (酥脆腐皮虾饼) is a Singaporean Chinese appetiser or side dish created by the Research and Development team in Singapore. It features premium whole prawns encased in a thin, delicate layer of beancurd skin (tofu skin / yuba) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a light, crunchy exterior with a tender, juicy prawn filling. The dish is typically served as a side dish or starter at Chinese-style restaurants and zi char stalls in Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 207 kcal, 4.05 g protein, 0.38 g fat, and 45.38 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium (5 mg) and low dietary fibre (0.7 g). The dominant macronutrient is refined carbohydrate from the wheat starch-based beancurd skin wrapper. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 67 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 15 per 50 g serving. Clinically, the dish is low concern for hypertension due to negligible sodium, but high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high refined carbohydrate load and low fibre. The deep-frying preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it moderate concern for cardiovascular disease and high concern for rheumatoid arthritis. It contains crustacean, soy, and gluten allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those on gluten-free diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010053), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for wheat starch-based deep-fried snacks.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010053) as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for this Singapore R&D original dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source record. 3. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on wheat starch-based deep-fried snack (spring roll / fried wonton wrapper) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in any approved source. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains prawn (halal-permissible) but preparation and kitchen certification are unknown; is_halal set to false pending certification. 5. This dish is an original creation of the HPB Singapore R&D team and does not have a traditional recipe lineage, which limits analogous nutrient cross-referencing.
